note: Script: Gothic|Decoration: initials (layout features); line fillings; long-line format; pen-flourished initials; rules (layout features);|Provenance: This leaf is from a portfolio compiled by The Foliophiles Inc. and issued as a limited edition under the title "Pages from the past: Original leaves from rare books and manuscripts."|Provenance: The set of leaves belonging to Ohio University is number 18 of 20 in "Portfolio set I: History of the written word."|Provenance: The transcribed labels accompanying these leaves were originally written by The Foliophiles, Inc., a now-defunct New York association of rare book dealers that was also known as the Society of Foliophiles.|Provenance: Between 1925 and 1968 The Foliophiles disbound hundreds of historical texts and assembled them into several thematic portfolio sets.|Provenance: These were then issued in “editions” of between 15 and 200 leaves. Universities across America subsequently purchased these portfolios as teaching tools.|Provenance: While a small number of the selected leaves come from other cultures, the vast majority are from European texts. The collecting and marketing practices of The Foliophiles reflect the values of the members of the association and the contemporary American rare book market.|Provenance: Original descriptions, therefore, may contain outdated language, speculation, or inaccurate information.|The following text is based on the original seller's label. FROM A THIRTEENTH CENTURY MANUSCRIPT, being a liturgical book, the Psalterium, similar to portions of the modern missal|This example is typical of the period when small manuscripts first came into vogue, and the large bold script of the Twelfth Century had given place to fine, compact gothic characters as seen on this page|The small initial letters with their neat outlines nevertheless are rather elaborately filigreed and a delight to the eye, as well as being unmistakably French in origin|The vellum, though showing the wear of 700 years, is of fine quality|Note the imaginative and artistic "space-filler designs."  © 1964, T.F.I.|Pages from the Past, Set I: History of the written word|This leaf - the front (recto) and back (verso) of a single sheet of velum removed from a book which has been deconstructed - was drawn from a psalter composed in France in the 12th century AD.|This leaf measures 4.3 by 5 inches, smaller than a trade paperback novel.|The text is an example of Gothic script, a bookhand that emphasizes sharp vertical lines and which sports a distinctive diamond shaped period.|The scribe has utilized vivid red and blue inks to form the capitals.|Red ink would have been produced using cinnabar, a compound containing mercury - making the scribal habit of licking the bristles of a brush to bring them to a point particularly hazardous!|The psalter is a liturgical work that first came into use in the early sixth century AD.|Psalters contain the biblical Book of Psalms, arranged in a deliberate order to facilitate Christian worship.|Medieval psalters were arranged according to the Rule of Saint Benedict.|The psalter would be performed as part of the Liturgy of the Hours, a series of eight points - the canonical ‘hours’ -  throughout each day during which monks would recite psalms.|This particular leaf contains the text of Psalm 68:22-28, which would have been recited by monks following the Benedictine rule at Matins on Wednesdays - during the night, typically around 2 AM.

The collecting and marketing practices of The Foliophiles reflect the values of the members of the association and the contemporary American rare book market.|Provenance: Original descriptions, therefore, may contain outdated language, speculation, or inaccurate information.|The following text is based on the original seller's label. FROM A GERMAN MANUSCRIPT on vellum|circa 1450, written in an upright, careful gothic German|A religious work|Such manuscripts served as the fountain of all learning until the advent of printing|thereafter to die out in a few score years|Of the many combinations of paper and vellum, printing and manuscript, only the printing on paper has survived today to serve as a recorder of history|The blue and red initial letters have retained clarity despite the hard usage to which this little book was obviously put|© 1964, T.F.I.|Pages from the Past, Set I: History of the written word|This leaf - the front (recto) and back (verso) of a single sheet of velum removed from a book which has been deconstructed - is drawn from a religious text manufactured in Germany around 1450 AD.|The leaf itself measures 5.75 by 4 inches, making it smaller than a trade paperback.|The text is an example of Gothic script, a bookhand that emphasizes sharp vertical lines and which sports a distinctive diamond shaped period.|The scribe has utilized vivid red and blue inks to form the capitals.|Of note on the verso are two duller capitals, likely written in terre verte or “Green Earth,” a dull green pigment produced from clay containing natural aluminum, magnesium, and iron silicates.|The psalter is a liturgical work that first came into use in the early sixth century AD.|Psalters contain the biblical Book of Psalms, arranged in a deliberate order to facilitate Christian worship.|Medieval psalters were arranged according to the Rule of Saint Benedict.|The psalter would be performed as part of the Liturgy of the Hours, a series of eight points - the canonical ‘hours’ -  throughout each day during which monks would recite psalms.|This particular leaf contains the text of Psalm 39:14-40:8, which would have been recited by monks following the Benedictine rule at Matins on Mondays - during the night, typically around 2 AM.

FROM A MANUSCRIPT KORAN, executed in a clear, firm hand in the style of chirography known as Naskhi|The paper, of course, was hand-made, and like most Oriental papers has been carefully sized and polished|The calligraphist wrote with a reed|Although this interesting MS|contains no record of place or date, it is probably Egyptian in origin, and undoubtedly of the Sixteenth Century|The language is Arabic, for no matter where a Koran is transcribed or printed, according to Muhammadan law it must adhere to the language of the adored Prophet|Arabic is a Semitic language, derived from the Aramean alphabet, and its gradual evolution, as a philologist puts it, "into a curious 'short-hand', with its many ambiguous abbreviations of the historic letters, is one of the most remarkable transformations known in the history of calligraphy"|And no less remarkable, it may be added, was the swift spread of this once insignificant dialect, unaided by the printing press, to a commanding position among the great languages of the world|Arabic, like Persian, is read from right to left|© 1926, T.F.I.|Pages from the Past, Set I: History of the written word

FROM A PERSIAN MANUSCRIPT of the early Seventeenth Century, viz., the Shahnama of Firdausi, probably the greatest, as it is the longest poem in the Persian language|Firdausi lived in the Tenth and Eleventh Centuries, and finished this, his chief work, about the year 1010|He has been likened to Homer, but the two poet-chroniclers were as different in their methods as it was possible to be, Firdausi's masterpiece being quite loose in structure, with innumerable personal references and egotistical digressions|Yet it embraces the whole mythological history of the Iranians and celebrates the achievements of their kings and rulers from earliest times to the invasion of the Saracens, a period of more than 3,600 years|This manuscript page, with its exquisite, minute decorations, shows in what high esteem Firdausi was held|The calligraphy, in the style known as Shekasteh Nastaliq, is no less perfect than the illuminations, and the manuscript in its entirety represented years of the most exacting labor, such as only an Oriental craftsman would have the will or the skill to perform|© 1926, T.F.I.|Pages from the Past, Set I: History of the written word|This leaf from the Shahnameh by Firdawsi (940 – 1019/1025), also known as Firdousi, is about the letter of Khosrow Parviz (King of Persia, Sassanid period) to the Roman (Byzantine) emperor.|During that period, 7th CE, both the Byzantine Empire and the Sassanid Dynasty of Persia were indeed powerful.|They engaged in prolonged conflicts, particularly the Byzantine-Sassanid Wars, which significantly weakened both empires.|These weakened states were subsequently vulnerable to the conquests by Arab Muslims, ultimately leading to the transformation of their territories into Islamic domains.

note: Decoration: Illuminations (painting); Initials (layout features); Musical notation; Rubrics;|Provenance: This leaf is from a portfolio compiled by The Foliophiles Inc. and issued as a limited edition under the title "Pages from the past: Original leaves from rare books and manuscripts."|Provenance: The set of leaves belonging to Ohio University is number 18 of 20 in "Portfolio set I: History of the written word."|Provenance: The transcribed labels accompanying these leaves were originally written by The Foliophiles, Inc., a now-defunct New York association of rare book dealers that was also known as the Society of Foliophiles.|Provenance: Between 1925 and 1968 The Foliophiles disbound hundreds of historical texts and assembled them into several thematic portfolio sets.|Provenance: These were then issued in “editions” of between 15 and 200 leaves. Universities across America subsequently purchased these portfolios as teaching tools.|Provenance: While a small number of the selected leaves come from other cultures, the vast majority are from European texts. The collecting and marketing practices of The Foliophiles reflect the values of the members of the association and the contemporary American rare book market.|Provenance: Original descriptions, therefore, may contain outdated language, speculation, or inaccurate information.|The following text is based on the original seller's label. FROM A SPANISH ANTIPHONARY of the Seventeenth Century, typical of the Catholic choir books of that period, particularly in Spain|The Antiphonary, or Antiphonal Book, formerly known as a Graduale, came into use in the early Middle Ages with the dual choir|For a time it contained all the liturgical songs, but later became restricted to the Introit, the Epistles, the Offertory and the Communion|The unusual size of some of these Spanish "missals," was possibly due to the gloom of the churches, especially in Barcelona and the South|hence also the plain, bold lettering and musical notation|Forced thus by necessity to make words and music readable at considerable distances in the semi­darkness, the monks took pleasure, not to say pride, in elaborating unimportant initials, as will be seen in this example|The evolution of the live line staff makes an interesting study|When first introduced it was restricted to secular music, the four line staff to church music|and while the latter persisted almost to modern times, the five staves assumed the ascendancy from the Sixteenth Century onwards|The student will recognize the square note with a tail as the long sound, the square note without a tail as the breve, and the lozenge-shaped note as the semi-breve|also he will notice the absence of some of the most important devices in modern musical nomenclature, and marvel at the instinctive skill necessary to bridge these omissions|This manuscript was purchased in Seville|© 1965, T.F.I.|Pages from the Past, Set I: History of the written word

In an area of the world where paper and even leather rots almost overnight, strips of palm have long  been used as a writing material|Note the two holes in the  leaf where a vine cord bound the book and allowed the pages  to be turned|The "colophon" states that this translation was  completed on the 7th waxing of the month of Tawthalin of the  Burmese year 1237 (September 1875)|The circular characters  are first inscribed on the leaf with a sharp instrument, such as an iron stylus,  then an ink of oil and charcoal is wiped over the characters to make them legible|The Burmese round characters developed because the thin fragile leaf of palm  would not take inscribing where long straight lines might split the fiber|© 1965, T.F.I.|Pages from the Past, Set I: History of the written word

FROM A MANUSCRIPT GULISTAN, or Rose Garden, of Sa’di, one of the most famous and  beautiful poems in the Persian language|The date corresponds to 1563 of the Christian era|On the margins, running concurrently, are the Ghazals, or Lyrics, of Sa’di|The calligraphy is in the  Shekasteh Nasta’liq style, and while not of great distinction, shows painstaking care and precision|The paper, like many of the preceding examples,  is of typical Oriental quality, made from vegetable fibres and linen rags, sised according to one of many secret processes, and polished by rubbing,  probably with a glass ball or shell|It is interesting to note that Persia learned paper making from the  Chinese, and that Samarkand was famous for its  paper as early as the 8th Century.|Specimens of Eastern Manuscripts and Printing|Gulistān, a landmark of Persian literature, is the most influential work of prose by the poet Saadi Shīrāzī (1210-1291/1292), written in 1258 CE.|It consists of a collection of poems and stories, often quoted for its wisdom. FROM A SANSKRIT MANUSCRIPT, probably of the early 19th Century, also written in  Devanagari or “town script”|The formation of  the characters, as well as the illuminations, show the hand of a Kashmiri scribe|This book contains selections from the Mahabharata and other Indian mythopoeic legends, and may loosely be compared  to a Latin Breviary|The Mahabharata is the oldest, and by far the longest, of the Sanskrit epics, and were it strictly an epic poem, could be called  the longest in the world|But of its one hundred  thousand couplets about four-fifths are of such a didactic nature that “the work has taken the place,” to quote Professor Macdonnel, “of a moral cyclopaedia in Indian literature”|Embedded in  this jungle of history, legend and ethical dissertations, however, are the beautiful romances of Nala and Damayanti, and the Bhagavad-gita or “Song of the Adorable One”, both well-known throughout the cultured world|While Hindu calligraphy and illumination never reached the exquisite refinement that characterizes that art in Muhammadan countries, one can readily see that Sanskrit  characters possess peculiar dignity and strength, calling for the robust treatment and striking color contrasts exemplified on this page.|Specimens of Eastern Manuscripts and Printing

EGE.|This composite text includes the Dialogues of Pope Gregory I (St.Gregory the Great, 540- 604 A.D.), which are largely autobiographical, and his writings on the lives and miracles of the early Italian Church Fathers|The book hand used is known as lettre bâtarde, a semi-cursive hand closely related to the everyday writing used by the people|Many French and Flemish printing types were based on similar bâtarde hands|The writing was done with comparative speed|the even tone and the exact alignment of the right hand margin, as well as the beauty of individual letters, are admirable|The long ascenders in the upper line were borrowed from the legal documents of that day|Many printers followed the practice shown here of emphasizing the tone of the first word or two in the beginning of a paragraph|It was usually done without varying the style of the letters, while here we see angular gothic used in the first third of the line, followed by the bâtarde script.|Fifty original leaves from medieval manuscripts, Western Europe, XII-XVI century
